key_insight: Nanoscratch-induced martensitic transformation in 316H stainless steel follows a stress-dependent sequence, with hydrostatic compression favoring γ→ε and shear under sliding promoting ε→α′, yielding a local γ→ε→α′ pathway in the tribolayer.
engineering_relevance: Understanding how contact pressure and shear select martensitic pathways can help design stainless steels and hardfacing alloys with better wear, galling, and surface durability.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Spatial mapping of evolving strain and phase distributions beneath a single asperity
Finite element modelling to interpret pressure, constraint, and shear-driven transformation pathways
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatially resolved experimental phase maps and strain fields, supported by finite element modeling that links hydrostatic constraint and shear to the observed martensitic phase distribution.
watch_item: The conclusions are drawn from a single-contact nanoscratch geometry, so translation to complex real-world multi-asperity tribological conditions may require additional validation.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
